response:
Imagine you walk into a restaurant and sit down. You can’t go into the kitchen and cook your own food. Instead, the restaurant gives you a menu showing exactly what you can ask for. When you pick something, you tell the waiter. The waiter carries your request to the kitchen, and when the food is ready, the waiter brings it back to your table.

An API is like that waiter and menu combined. It is the go-between that lets one thing ask another thing for something specific, and get exactly what it asked for, without either one needing to see how the other works on the inside.

Here is a real example. Say you have a weather app on your phone. That app does not own thermometers or fly balloons into the sky. A weather company does. Your app uses an API—the “waiter”—to ask that company for today’s forecast. The company sends the information back through the API, and your app shows you the temperature and the little sun icon. Your app never steps into the weather company’s kitchen, and the weather company never touches your phone. They just use the API to pass the information back and forth safely and neatly.
